one_line_takeaway: The first systematic security study of on-device mobile LLM agents shows every one of nine deployed agents is exploitable (avg 6.3/11 attack surfaces), with UI-overlay attacks near-universal and vulnerability profiles determined by each agent's perception/decision architecture.
key_finding: All nine agents were vulnerable to multiple attack vectors; on average each agent was susceptible to 6.3 of 11 attack surfaces, with no agent fully protected. The most vulnerable was AppAgent at 8/11. GUI manipulation was the broadest surface: Transparent Overlay and Pop-up Interference each succeeded against 7 of 9 agents. A clear architectural split emerged: OEM system-level agents (Agent-A..D) were immune to LLM-layer attacks (rule/logic-oriented, low LLM reliance) but were the only agents vulnerable to System-layer Package Name Forgery and Deeplink Forgery (all 4 susceptible), reflecting missing intent/signature validation. Susceptibility to Viewtree Interference tracked perception design: 100% (10/10) against viewtree-reliant AutoDroid and DroidBot-GPT but 0/10 against the pure screenshot/OCR Mobile-Agent and Mobile-Agent-v2 — while Image Forgery for UI Elements inverted this (9/10 on vision-based Mobile-Agent-v2). A composite end-to-end exploit chaining Transparent Overlay + Malicious Instruction + Prompt Injection via Display against Mobile-Agent-v2 extracted a bank-card password from agent memory. Preliminary hardening of Mobile-Agent-v2 (tighter action-to-viewtree binding, instruction/screen-text alignment, pre-execution screen validation) sharply cut success rates but did not fully close prompt injection or glitch-token vectors.
core_contribution: The paper presents what the authors claim is the first systematic security analysis of mobile LLM agents (on-device smartphone automation agents that decompose natural-language tasks into UI/system actions). Its own contributions are: (1) a structured characterization of three deployment classes of mobile LLM agents (OEM system-level agents e.g. Honor YOYO/Vivo Blue Heart; third-party universal agents e.g. Zhipu AutoGLM; and PC-connected emerging agent frameworks e.g. Alibaba Mobile-Agent, Tencent AppAgent), derived by reverse-engineering nine agents; (2) a taxonomy of 11 distinct attack surfaces organized across three interaction layers (LLM layer, GUI layer, System layer) spanning the agent's perception-reasoning-actuation lifecycle; (3) AgentScan, a semi-automated client-server testing framework that injects crafted attacks (malicious APKs, floating-window overlays, forged intents/deeplinks, log monitoring) at precise execution stages; and (4) an empirical evaluation of nine deployed agents plus a preliminary hardening of Mobile-Agent-v2. Note the LLM-layer and some GUI/System threats are explicitly adapted from prior work (prompt injection, glitch tokens, clickjacking overlays, intent/deeplink hijacking); the paper's novelty is contextualizing and systematically testing them against mobile agents, not inventing those primitives.
threat_model: The attacker controls a benign-looking third-party app already installed on the victim's device. The app needs no root or administrative access, but may obtain sensitive permissions such as SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW (floating windows) via social engineering or user consent. With this foothold the attacker injects misleading inputs, overlays UI elements, registers forged package names/deeplinks, or reads system logs to manipulate the agent's behavior or exfiltrate user data. The agent is assumed to run with elevated/system privileges and to autonomously execute actions without per-step human oversight, which amplifies impact.
limitations: Authors acknowledge: (1) Limited scope — only specific/typical attack types are covered; other real-world vectors and dynamic/evolving agents are not explored, limiting generalizability. (2) Model limitations — GPT-4o was fixed as the single backend for fairness, but different LLMs could yield different attack outcomes; the study measures the agent's defenses, not the model's intrinsic robustness. Additional reviewer-noted limits: only nine agents; four are anonymized (Agent-A..D) so results aren't independently reproducible on those; 'semi-automated' framework still needs manual instruction adaptation; success is per-attack on isolated stages rather than realistic full-task compromise (addressed only by a single composite case study); and the hardening evaluation is a preliminary single-agent proof-of-concept, not a general defense.

datasets_used: Android in the Wild (AITW), Google's real-world Android task dataset — source of task templates, manually adapted into a 44-task baseline instruction set

Custom Malicious-Instruction-for-Agent dataset: 10 baseline malicious tasks (e.g., unauthorized data access, silent message sending, file deletion) optimized with social-engineering strategies (Fake Consent, Gamification, Authority Bias, Emergency Simulation, Role-Playing Trap)
10 representative glitch tokens replicated from Li et al.'s Glitch Token study, embedded into baseline instructions
quantitative_results: 9 agents evaluated across 11 attack vectors in 3 interaction dimensions (LLM/GUI/System)
Average vulnerability: 6.3 of 11 attack vectors per agent; no agent fully protected
Most vulnerable agent: AppAgent, 8 of 11 attack surfaces; system-level agents fewest at 5
Malicious Instructions: effective on 5 agents, success 5/10-9/10 (AppAgent 9/10); all four system-level agents immune
Glitch Tokens: impacted 5 agents, up to 4/10 (AppAgent)
Transparent Overlay: succeeded on 7 of 9 agents (10/10 on AutoDroid, Mobile-Agent, Mobile-Agent-v2, AppAgent, DroidBot-GPT, Agent-C, Agent-D)
Pop-up Interference: succeeded on 7 of 9 agents
Prompt Injection via Display: effective on all five agent frameworks (Mobile-Agent-v2 10/10, Mobile-Agent 9/10, Agent-D vision 8/10); failed on system-level agents
Viewtree Interference: 10/10 on AutoDroid and DroidBot-GPT and Agent-A/B/C; 0/10 on Mobile-Agent and Mobile-Agent-v2 (pure OCR/vision)
Image Forgery for UI Elements: Mobile-Agent-v2 9/10, Mobile-Agent 7/10; system-level agents unaffected
Image Forgery for APP: 10/10 against Mobile-Agent, Mobile-Agent-v2, AppAgent (no dedicated launcher); ineffective on all others
Package Name Forgery and Deeplink Forgery: succeeded on all four system-level agents (Agent-A..D) exclusively

Mobile-Agent-v2 hardening (unprotected -> protected): Malicious Instructions 5/10->1/10, Glitch Tokens 2/10->0/10, Image Forgery UI 9/10->2/10, Image Forgery APP 10/10->fail, Prompt Injection via Display 10/10->2/10, Transparent Overlay 10/10->fail, Pop-up Interference 10/10->fail
Two OEM device vendors acknowledged the responsible disclosures
baselines_compared: No prior security-analysis baseline exists (paper claims to be the first); comparison is across the nine tested agents themselves: AutoDroid, Mobile-Agent, Mobile-Agent-v2, AppAgent, DroidBot-GPT, and anonymized system-level/third-party agents Agent-A, Agent-B, Agent-C, Agent-D
Before-vs-after security-protection comparison of Mobile-Agent-v2 (Table IV) serves as the internal defense baseline
Agent-D evaluated in two configurations (Vision-Based vs Structure-Based) to contrast perception-source robustness
